one_line_takeaway: In a NERC-grounded jailbreak benchmark against three commercial LLMs acting as smart-grid compliance assistants, DeepInception's nested-narrative attack achieved up to 100% attack success against Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite and 91.67% against GPT-4o mini, while Claude 3.5 Haiku had 0% ASR across all 2916+570 one-shot trials -- and even a single reworded keyword measurably raised simpler attacks' success rates.
key_finding: E1 overall ASR was 33.1% (966/2916 successful attacks) across all models and methods. DeepInception was by far the most effective attack (63.2% aggregate ASR in E1, per Fig. 4: 0.632 vs BitBypass 0.234 vs Baseline 0.129 -- nearly 5x Baseline). Per-model E1 ASR: Claude 3.5 Haiku 0.00% (0/972, immune to every attack at every temperature), Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite 55.04% (most vulnerable; up to 100% ASR against DeepInception at temperature 0.1, 98.1% failure rate against DeepInception overall), GPT-4o mini 44.34% (moderately susceptible; up to 91.67% ASR against DeepInception). E2 (Baseline + BitBypass only, reworded keywords) yielded overall ASR 30.6%, with aggregate attack-level ASR of 0.392 for BitBypass vs 0.220 for Baseline (BitBypass ~78% more effective than Baseline in E2). Gemini's Baseline ASR nearly doubled from E1 to E2 (peaking at 51.58% at temperature 0.5) and its BitBypass ASR peaked at 77.89% at temperature 0.5. A matched-scenario comparison (Table IV, E1-filtered vs E2) showed the average Baseline ASR rose from 20.27% to 21.99% (+8.5% relative) and Gemini's BitBypass ASR rose by 10.76 percentage points, demonstrating that a single-word swap in malicious phrasing ('fake' to 'counterfeit') measurably raises attack success. Temperature had minimal effect in E1 (<5% variation) but a larger effect in E2. By NERC category, E1 ASR was fairly uniform across CIP/EOP/TOP (slightly higher for CIP and TOP), while in E2 the more explicit wording shifted the most-vulnerable category to TOP standards.
core_contribution: The paper's own contribution (not related work) is an empirical, two-phase jailbreaking benchmark for LLMs deployed as compliance/operations assistants in electric grid control rooms. The authors built scenarios derived from nine NERC Reliability Standards (three each from EOP, TOP, CIP categories) and tested three commercial LLMs (GPT-4o mini, Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite, Claude 3.5 Haiku) against three jailbreak methods (a direct Baseline prompt, the BitBypass obfuscation attack, and the DeepInception nested-narrative attack), each drawn from prior published techniques (BitBypass and DeepInception are cited/reused methods, not novel to this paper) but applied here for the first time to a NERC-grounded smart-grid compliance-assistant setting. Experiment E1 was the broad sweep across all 3 models x 3 attacks x 9 scenarios x 3 temperatures x 3 repetitions (2916 total outputs). Experiment E2 was a targeted follow-up that reworded the malicious keyword in Baseline/BitBypass prompts to be more explicitly nefarious (e.g., 'fake' to 'counterfeit') to test refusal thresholds, run only on Baseline and BitBypass across a subset of scenarios (570 outputs per model, 5 repetitions per prompt).
threat_model: Insider adversary: an authorized user with direct access to the LLM assistant's prompt interface (e.g., grid operator, shift supervisor, technician), motivated by convenience or malice, submitting a single-interaction (one-shot) malicious prompt to elicit guidance that violates a NERC standard the assistant is meant to enforce. The LLM is assumed integrated into workflows such as report generation, operational planning, and security management, with the relevant NERC standard supplied as a system-prompt-adjacent file reference and a compliance instruction ('Adhere to XYZ-123') appended. The threat model explicitly excludes external/remote adversaries, privilege escalation, code injection, or system hacking -- attacks require at most moderate prompt-engineering skill. The authors note real-world attackers would likely have a persistent chat interface to iterate past an initial refusal, but their measurements are one-shot only.
limitations: Authors' own stated limitations: only 3 models and 3 attack methods were tested via API/Python SDK in a simulated (not live production) setup; results will age as new models/attacks emerge; the paper is zero-shot only and does not consider task-specific fine-tuning, retrieval-augmented generation, or other real-world deployment variations; only prompt-based jailbreaking was studied, not broader risks like data poisoning or supply-chain attacks; attacks were evaluated one-shot even though the authors' own threat model acknowledges a persistent chat interface would let a real attacker iterate past initial hedged refusals, so reported ASR may understate real-world risk; pass/fail labeling was manual (human-judged), with no reported inter-rater reliability figure; whether Claude 3.5 Haiku's 0% ASR reflects Anthropic-specific alignment or a more general property of models with stronger reasoning/'thinking' capabilities is explicitly left as an open question, not resolved by the paper.

datasets_used: 9 custom scenarios derived from NERC Reliability Standards: EOP-004-4, EOP-008-2, EOP-011-4, TOP-001-6, TOP-002-5, TOP-010-1(i), CIP-005-7, CIP-006-6, CIP-007-6 (authors' own constructed prompts/case studies, not a pre-existing public dataset)
quantitative_results: E1 per-attack aggregate ASR: DeepInception 63.2% (0.632), BitBypass 23.4% (0.234), Baseline 12.9% (0.129)
E1 per-model overall ASR: Claude 3.5 Haiku 0.00%, GPT-4o mini 44.34%, Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite 55.04%
E1 Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite vs DeepInception: up to 100.0% ASR at temperature 0.1, 98.15% at 0.5, 96.30% at 0.95
E2 per-attack aggregate ASR: BitBypass 39.2% (0.392), Baseline 22.0% (0.220)
E2 Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite BitBypass ASR peak: 77.89% at temperature 0.5
E2 Gemini 2.0 Flash-Lite Baseline ASR peak: 51.58% at temperature 0.5

Matched-scenario comparison (Table IV): average Baseline ASR rose from 20.27% (E1-filtered) to 21.99% (E2), an 8.5% relative increase; Gemini's BitBypass ASR increased by 10.76 percentage points from E1-filtered to E2
Claude 3.5 Haiku: 0.00% ASR in every cell of Tables II, III, and IV (all attacks, all temperatures, both experiments)
baselines_compared: Baseline attack (direct, unconcealed malicious prompt) -- used as the paper's own control condition, not an external baseline system
BitBypass jailbreak method (Nakka & Saxena, EACL Findings 2026) -- binary-bitstream keyword obfuscation, applied as-is from prior work
DeepInception jailbreak method (Li et al., arXiv:2311.03191) -- nested-narrative psychological manipulation, applied as-is from prior work
Three temperature settings (0.1, 0.5, 0.95) compared against each other per model/attack as an internal ablation, not a baseline model
